items.myth: A 99% accurate test means a positive result implies a 99% chance of having the condition.
items.reality: Test accuracy is P(+|disease) and P(−|healthy), not P(disease|+). Bayes' theorem combines these with disease prevalence; rare conditions yield low posteriors even for good tests.
items.example: With 0.1% prevalence, 99% sensitivity, 95% specificity: P(disease|+) ≈ 1.9%, not 99%.

items.myth: Bayes' theorem requires assuming events A and B are independent.
items.reality: The theorem holds for any dependent events with P(B) > 0. Independence is an optional simplifying assumption in models like Naive Bayes, not a requirement of the formula.
items.example: In the card example, King and face card are dependent, yet P(K|face) = 1/3 follows directly from Bayes.

items.myth: The prior P(A) cancels out or does not matter when data is strong.
items.reality: The prior always enters the numerator P(B|A)P(A); with limited or ambiguous data, priors materially shift posteriors. Only overwhelming likelihood ratios overwhelm a prior.
items.example: Two equally likely coins after 3 heads: posteriors tilt toward bias, but with 3 flips the shift is modest; with 3000 flips the prior matters far less.

items.myth: Bayes' theorem only applies to Bayesian statisticians, not frequentist ML.
items.reality: The identity is a probability law used everywhere—Naive Bayes classifiers, calibration analysis, and diagnostic reasoning all rely on it regardless of philosophical stance on priors.
items.example: sklearn's GaussianNB predicts argmax P(class|features) using Bayes' rule even when you never specify a subjective prior—empirical class frequencies serve as P(class).
